title: Fragments: July 21
content_html:
With this post, I’ll wrap up my notes from the second Future of Software Development Retreat. But before I do, I should note that the full Thoughtworks report on the retreat is now available. They have five headline findings:

  • Code generation is no longer the bottleneck — verification is.
  • ‘Harness engineering’ is emerging as a distinct, ownable discipline.
  • Organizations are colliding with a real apprenticeship crisis.
  • The executive/engineer expectation gap is a bigger risk than any technical limitation.
  • Legacy modernization is the clearest, most defensible near-term value pool.

 ❄                ❄

A session convened around the mismatch of views about using LLMs between engineers using it and the C-suite and boards that were calling for it. The concern is that boards are looking at promised productivity gains, and not concerned enough about the risks, particularly about security.

This was illustrated by one tale of a company that used ML-trained software to optimize the replacement of air filters on their field equipment. They were pleased to see that they were able to change the air filters less frequently, saving them $50 million. But the problem was the ML models were trained on equipment used in the desert, while their equipment was used in the arctic. Air filters in the desert deal with dust, but in the arctic the thing to remove is mosquitoes. There’s an important difference here, mosquitoes rot, and enough decaying mosquitoes is a serious fire risk. Fires from such dead mosquitoes around infrequently replaced air filters cost the company $100 billion.

Now such a tale could told of many situations without AI in the mix. Plenty of human situations have gone wrong when solutions are applied in a new context (which is why context is such a key word among pattern-writers). But the tale does remind us to be wary of an AI’s suggestions, and to always think of how to build sensors to provide rapid feedback.

Engineers particularly worry about the risks when citizen developers start vibe coding. In many ways, of course, this isn’t new. I.T. folks often worry about how many important business decisions are based on spreadsheets, that are built with little control, testing, or assessment of data quality. Vibe-coding amplifies these concerns, so companies need a range of controls to guard against security breaches. Some folks have made a point of raising issues at board level, running threat modeling session with board members to introduce them to the risks. Vibe-coded applications need to be put in separate infrastructure, which deterministic controls over data access to tame the lethal trifecta. One company encouraged widespread vibe-coding from citizen developers but recoiled from the problems of the huge shadow IT that emerged - they are now looking to build a platform to help control this work without stifling the useful tools that were produced.

Part of the problem here may be simple experience with LLMs. Many in management find LLMs do a decent job of preparing management reports. Or summarizing management reports prepared by other LLMs. Given this they naturally think LLMs must do a decent job of programming too. My anti-management self has to mention Kelsey Hightower’s observation:

The less busy work you have the less appealing these Al tools are

One possible antidote to this: get the legal department involved. They see LLMs doing a poor job, and appreciate the risks involved.

 ❄                ❄

Most folks I talk to, both at the retreat and outside, recognize we are in some form of bubble. Technological advances like this almost always come with economic bubbles, and in the future we will all look back at this, and shake our heads saying we knew there was so much froth. But while it’s easy to see that there is a bubble, it’s hard to see how long it will run or what will emerge after the pop. After all the dotcom bubble was clearly recognized as such… in 1995. We can happily point at those companies that failed (Webvan, pets.com) but need to then acknowledge those that survived (Amazon).

Most of those at the retreat were old enough to have lived through the dotcom bubble and crash, but one such grey-hair pointed out an interesting difference. Back then we were excited about what the future would bring, and we saw lots of new things being built. There’s much less of that, this time around. Most people are wary of what the AI bubble is creating. Partly this may stem from the reality that followed the dotcom hope. Social media may be everywhere, but do we think it’s actually improved our lives that much, even if (especially if?) we use so much of it?

We hear so much about the incredibly productive things we can do with agentic programming, but has anyone noticed a flood of wonderful applications built with it? Or have we noticed a significant improvement in common applications from the big AI boosters such as Google or Microsoft?

This may be another factor in the board-vs-engineer divide. Most of what’s driving adoption of AI at the moment is cost-cutting, and it mostly the boards that get excited by cost-cutting. Perhaps the increasing concerns about token costs will temper the eagerness.

 ❄                ❄

Folks are finding LLMs helpful in operations: with a good event stream from observability tools, an agent finds anomalies much faster. One of the problems with citizen-developer apps, is that they often don’t provide good observability, since the citizen-developers don’t think to ask for it. The agents ability to look at the event stream does pose governance questions, as often such event streams contain a lot of sensitive information.

Reinforcing what I’d heard in Utah, more people agreed that LLMs are valuable for operations folks to help them understand what the code does. Cross matching code and event traces helps them assist humans to find what happened when things go wrong. Agents are particularly handy with repeated incidents, as they can collate lots of information from different cases and present it to the human teams.

Getting agents to auto-remediate moves us to the next level of capabilities and concerns. It’s vital that agents carefully document all their actions when they do fixes. We also need to ensure there is feedback to the development team so they can learn. Agents don’t learn, the best they can do is update the context.

There was a sense that many people over-estimate the capability of agents to deal with incidents. Such people think of incident resolution as a simple, linear process. But it’s rarely that, instead there’s a lot of surprises and adaptation needed. Humans are good with that, but LLMs are not.

One of the perils of agent-developed code is their habit of inserting features that were never asked for. One team spent three days trying to figure out such an unrequested feature, trying to figure out who had requested it and if anyone wanted to keep it.

 ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄

A group of law professors carried an interesting experiment to judge how well an LLM can provide short answers to student questions. They created a batch of forty questions in contract law and asked the professors, plus a couple of LLMs, to provide answers. To evaluate the LLM answers they showed professors pairs of answers - one human, one LLM - and asked them which response they would prefer to deliver to a student.

Professors rated LLMs far higher than their peers (average win rate = 75.33%), with models performing similarly to the best instructor. LLM responses were also rarely flagged as harmful (3.53%, vs 12.06% for professors).

This reminds me of the distinction I mentioned in a recent fragment between interactional and contributory expertise.

 ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄

A few days ago Unmesh Joshi published an article here about his experiences using DSLs to enable more reliable use of LLMs. Responses to this included a pointer to an article by Spender Nelson that related similar impressions.

DSLs like this hit a lot of sweet spots for LLMs. You can make them extremely token efficient, and enforce hard security boundaries. You can translate high-level LLM intent into a ton of deterministic code, ensuring good behavior and guardrails at the (custom) compiler level.

And Large Language Models are very good at learning and working with DSLs. Maybe this shouldn’t come as a surprise; they are language models after all. A small bit of documentation generally is enough to set them off and running, and reasonable error messages let them course-correct even when they go wrong.

He describes a couple of examples from their use: a query language for data lakes that takes into account security and authorization issues, and a little expression language to make it easier to create safe SQL where clauses.

One of the biggest barriers to using DSLs, particularly external DSLs, is building a parser and tooling. LLMs make this much easier. That said, my sense is that it’s the semantic model that underpins the DSL is what really matters, and the DSL is one projection of that model. LLMs may help us explore other ways to project that model in interesting ways.

 ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄

In recent weeks I’ve been noticing the stench of LLM-speak more and more. It’s not just the common tells, it’s a sense of LLM miasma that pervades the prose. I’ve noticed it’s increasingly eliciting a visceral reaction, after a couple of paragraphs I just want to dismiss the entire article out of hand. For some of these, it was necessary for me to hold my nose and wade through the whole text, but it was with an intellectual nausea which obscured the content, even increasing my desire to indulge in such an awful distraction as checking social media.

I wonder - is this just me that’s reacting so negatively to LLM-speak? Or do other people have a reaction that leads them to toss aside any prose that sets off their LLM-alarm?

One indicator that it’s not just me is this post from Jason Koebler that I highlighted a couple of months ago, where he observed how AI was breaking his brain:

People think things that are fake are real, things that are real are fake. Much has been written about “AI psychosis,” the nonspecific, nonscientific diagnosis given to people who have lost themselves to AI. Less has been said about the cognitive load of what other people’s AI use is doing to the rest of us, and the insidious nature of having to navigate an internet and a world where lazy AI has infiltrated everything. Our brains are now performing untold numbers of calculations per day: Is this AI? Do I care if it’s AI? Why does this sound or look or read so weird? Does this person just write like this? Is this a person at all?

A while ago, I was thinking that it was reasonable for folks who aren’t as committed to writing as I am to use an AI to help polish their prose. Now I’m turning to encouraging writers to reject it. That pervasive LLM-voice is just so common now, my sense is that it discredits the writing even before the reader has a chance to try to understand what is being said. I don’t think it’s good enough to ask the LLM to write a first draft and then tweak it. I’m not sure writers can edit the LLM-ness out of prose once it’s in there. I even worry about asking an LLM to suggest improvements, I think it’s just too easy to accept an LLM’s suggestions, and in the process trigger your readers’ LLM-antibodies.

Of course like most problems, it’s also an opportunity. Those who can get a distinctive human voice will get more visibility and credibility. But the question remains of how we can coach people to let out their true personality into their writing. Academic and corporate writing both tended to stifle engaging prose, LLMs are good amplifiers, and they will amplify this stifling. This is an even greater challenge for those for whom English is their second language (or indeed for many of my colleagues, their third or fourth). It’s too easy for me to neglect to think about a difficulty that I’ve never been able to face.

The most immediate advice I can give something I learned many years ago and shared last year - Say Your Writing.

Once you’ve got a reasonable draft, read it out loud. By doing this you’ll find bits that don’t sound right, and need to fix.

I always suggested this to help people get past sluggish prose, especially if they had spent too much time around academic or corporate writing. But now I think the need to Say Your Writing is even more important, in order to combat the insidious impact of AI. For most people, their speech patterns get closer to their real self, so verbalizing writing is the way to fight those forces that try to smooth away a writer’s individuality.

sentences.zh: 但这个故事确实提醒我们要警惕人工智能的建议，并始终思考如何构建传感器来提供快速反馈。

blocks.html_en: Engineers particularly worry about the risks when citizen developers start vibe coding. In many ways, of course, this isn’t new. I.T. folks often worry about how many important business decisions are based on spreadsheets, that are built with little control, testing, or assessment of data quality. Vibe-coding amplifies these concerns, so companies need a range of controls to guard against security breaches. Some folks have made a point of raising issues at board level, running threat modeling session with board members to introduce them to the risks. Vibe-coded applications need to be put in separate infrastructure, which deterministic controls over data access to tame the lethal trifecta. One company encouraged widespread vibe-coding from citizen developers but recoiled from the problems of the huge shadow IT that emerged - they are now looking to build a platform to help control this work without stifling the useful tools that were produced.

sentences.en: Engineers particularly worry about the risks when citizen developers start vibe coding.
sentences.zh: 当公民开发人员开始进行氛围编码时，工程师尤其担心风险。

sentences.en: One company encouraged widespread vibe-coding from citizen developers but recoiled from the problems of the huge shadow IT that emerged - they are now looking to build a platform to help control this work without stifling the useful tools that were produced.

blocks.html_en: Most folks I talk to, both at the retreat and outside, recognize we are in some form of bubble. Technological advances like this almost always come with economic bubbles, and in the future we will all look back at this, and shake our heads saying we knew there was so much froth. But while it’s easy to see that there is a bubble, it’s hard to see how long it will run or what will emerge after the pop. After all the dotcom bubble was clearly recognized as such… in 1995. We can happily point at those companies that failed (Webvan, pets.com) but need to then acknowledge those that survived (Amazon).

sentences.zh: 与我交谈过的大多数人，无论是在静修处还是在外面，都认识到我们正处于某种形式的泡沫之中。

sentences.en: Technological advances like this almost always come with economic bubbles, and in the future we will all look back at this, and shake our heads saying we knew there was so much froth.
sentences.zh: 像这样的技术进步几乎总是伴随着经济泡沫，未来我们都会回顾这一点，摇头说我们知道有这么多泡沫。

sentences.en: But while it’s easy to see that there is a bubble, it’s hard to see how long it will run or what will emerge after the pop.
sentences.zh: 但是，虽然很容易看出泡沫的存在，但很难看出泡沫会持续多久或泡沫破灭后会出现什么。

sentences.en: After all the dotcom bubble was clearly recognized as such…
sentences.zh: 毕竟互联网泡沫已被清楚地认识到……

sentences.en: We can happily point at those companies that failed (Webvan, pets.com) but need to then acknowledge those that survived (Amazon).
sentences.zh: 我们可以高兴地指出那些失败的公司（Webvan、pets.com），但需要承认那些幸存下来的公司（亚马逊）。

blocks.html_en: Most of those at the retreat were old enough to have lived through the dotcom bubble and crash, but one such grey-hair pointed out an interesting difference. Back then we were excited about what the future would bring, and we saw lots of new things being built. There’s much less of that, this time around. Most people are wary of what the AI bubble is creating. Partly this may stem from the reality that followed the dotcom hope. Social media may be everywhere, but do we think it’s actually improved our lives that much, even if (especially if?) we use so much of it?

sentences.en: Most of those at the retreat were old enough to have lived through the dotcom bubble and crash, but one such grey-hair pointed out an interesting difference.
sentences.zh: 参加静修会的大多数人年龄都足够大，经历过互联网泡沫和崩溃，但其中一位白发人士指出了一个有趣的差异。

sentences.en: Back then we were excited about what the future would bring, and we saw lots of new things being built.

sentences.en: There’s much less of that, this time around.
sentences.zh: 这次，这样的事情要少得多。

sentences.en: Most people are wary of what the AI bubble is creating.

sentences.zh: 我们用了这么多吗？

blocks.html_en: We hear so much about the incredibly productive things we can do with agentic programming, but has anyone noticed a flood of wonderful applications built with it? Or have we noticed a significant improvement in common applications from the big AI boosters such as Google or Microsoft?

sentences.en: We hear so much about the incredibly productive things we can do with agentic programming, but has anyone noticed a flood of wonderful applications built with it?
sentences.zh: 我们听说过很多关于使用代理编程可以实现的令人难以置信的高效事情，但是有人注意到用它构建了大量精彩的应用程序吗？

sentences.en: Or have we noticed a significant improvement in common applications from the big AI boosters such as Google or Microsoft?
sentences.zh: 或者我们是否注意到谷歌或微软等大型人工智能助推器对常见应用程序的显着改进？

blocks.html_en: This may be another factor in the board-vs-engineer divide. Most of what’s driving adoption of AI at the moment is cost-cutting, and it mostly the boards that get excited by cost-cutting. Perhaps the increasing concerns about token costs will temper the eagerness.

sentences.en: This may be another factor in the board-vs-engineer divide.
sentences.zh: 这可能是董事会与工程师分歧的另一个因素。

sentences.en: Most of what’s driving adoption of AI at the moment is cost-cutting, and it mostly the boards that get excited by cost-cutting.
sentences.zh: 目前推动人工智能采用的大部分因素是成本削减，而董事会对成本削减感到兴奋。

sentences.en: Perhaps the increasing concerns about token costs will temper the eagerness.
sentences.zh: 也许对代币成本日益增加的担忧会缓和这种渴望。

blocks.html_en: Folks are finding LLMs helpful in operations: with a good event stream from observability tools, an agent finds anomalies much faster. One of the problems with citizen-developer apps, is that they often don’t provide good observability, since the citizen-developers don’t think to ask for it. The agents ability to look at the event stream does pose governance questions, as often such event streams contain a lot of sensitive information.

sentences.en: Folks are finding LLMs helpful in operations: with a good event stream from observability tools, an agent finds anomalies much faster.
sentences.zh: 人们发现法学硕士对运营很有帮助：通过可观察性工具提供的良好事件流，代理可以更快地发现异常情况。

sentences.en: One of the problems with citizen-developer apps, is that they often don’t provide good observability, since the citizen-developers don’t think to ask for it.
sentences.zh: 公民开发者应用程序的问题之一是它们通常不能提供良好的可观察性，因为公民开发者不认为需要它。

sentences.en: The agents ability to look at the event stream does pose governance questions, as often such event streams contain a lot of sensitive information.
sentences.zh: 代理查看事件流的能力确实会带来治理问题，因为此类事件流通常包含大量敏感信息。

blocks.html_en: Reinforcing what I’d heard in Utah, more people agreed that LLMs are valuable for operations folks to help them understand what the code does. Cross matching code and event traces helps them assist humans to find what happened when things go wrong. Agents are particularly handy with repeated incidents, as they can collate lots of information from different cases and present it to the human teams.

sentences.en: Reinforcing what I’d heard in Utah, more people agreed that LLMs are valuable for operations folks to help them understand what the code does.
sentences.zh: 更多的人同意法学硕士对于运营人员来说很有价值，可以帮助他们理解代码的作用，这证实了我在犹他州听到的说法。

sentences.en: Cross matching code and event traces helps them assist humans to find what happened when things go wrong.
sentences.zh: 交叉匹配代码和事件跟踪可以帮助人们在出现问题时找到发生的情况。

sentences.en: Agents are particularly handy with repeated incidents, as they can collate lots of information from different cases and present it to the human teams.
sentences.zh: 特工在处理重复事件时特别得心应手，因为他们可以整理来自不同案例的大量信息并将其呈现给人类团队。

blocks.html_en: Getting agents to auto-remediate moves us to the next level of capabilities and concerns. It’s vital that agents carefully document all their actions when they do fixes. We also need to ensure there is feedback to the development team so they can learn. Agents don’t learn, the best they can do is update the context.

sentences.en: Getting agents to auto-remediate moves us to the next level of capabilities and concerns.
sentences.zh: 让代理自动修复将我们的能力和关注点提升到一个新的水平。

sentences.en: It’s vital that agents carefully document all their actions when they do fixes.
sentences.zh: 代理商在进行修复时仔细记录他们的所有操作，这一点至关重要。

sentences.en: We also need to ensure there is feedback to the development team so they can learn.
sentences.zh: 我们还需要确保向开发团队提供反馈，以便他们可以学习。

sentences.en: Agents don’t learn, the best they can do is update the context.
sentences.zh: 代理不会学习，他们能做的最好的事情就是更新上下文。

blocks.html_en: There was a sense that many people over-estimate the capability of agents to deal with incidents. Such people think of incident resolution as a simple, linear process. But it’s rarely that, instead there’s a lot of surprises and adaptation needed. Humans are good with that, but LLMs are not.

sentences.en: There was a sense that many people over-estimate the capability of agents to deal with incidents.
sentences.zh: 有一种感觉，许多人高估了特工处理事件的能力。

sentences.en: Such people think of incident resolution as a simple, linear process.
sentences.zh: 这些人认为事件解决是一个简单的线性过程。

sentences.en: But it’s rarely that, instead there’s a lot of surprises and adaptation needed.
sentences.zh: 但这种情况很少发生，相反，需要很多惊喜和适应。

sentences.en: Humans are good with that, but LLMs are not.
sentences.zh: 人类很擅长这一点，但法学硕士却不然。

blocks.html_en: One of the perils of agent-developed code is their habit of inserting features that were never asked for. One team spent three days trying to figure out such an unrequested feature, trying to figure out who had requested it and if anyone wanted to keep it.

sentences.en: One of the perils of agent-developed code is their habit of inserting features that were never asked for.
sentences.zh: 代理开发代码的危险之一是他们习惯于插入从未被要求的功能。

sentences.en: One team spent three days trying to figure out such an unrequested feature, trying to figure out who had requested it and if anyone wanted to keep it.
sentences.zh: 一个团队花了三天时间试图找出这样一个未被请求的功能，试图找出谁请求了它以及是否有人想保留它。

blocks.html_en: A group of law professors carried an interesting experiment to judge how well an LLM can provide short answers to student questions. They created a batch of forty questions in contract law and asked the professors, plus a couple of LLMs, to provide answers. To evaluate the LLM answers they showed professors pairs of answers - one human, one LLM - and asked them which response they would prefer to deliver to a student.

sentences.en: A group of law professors carried an interesting experiment to judge how well an LLM can provide short answers to student questions.
sentences.zh: 一群法学教授进行了一项有趣的实验，以判断法学硕士能够在多大程度上为学生的问题提供简短的答案。

sentences.en: They created a batch of forty questions in contract law and asked the professors, plus a couple of LLMs, to provide answers.
sentences.zh: 他们创建了一批四十个合同法问题，并要求教授和几位法学硕士提供答案。

sentences.en: To evaluate the LLM answers they showed professors pairs of answers - one human, one LLM - and asked them which response they would prefer to deliver to a student.
sentences.zh: 为了评估法学硕士的答案，他们向教授展示了一对答案——一个人，一个法学硕士——并询问他们更愿意向学生提供哪一个答案。

blocks.html_en: Professors rated LLMs far higher than their peers (average win rate = 75.33%), with models performing similarly to the best instructor. LLM responses were also rarely flagged as harmful (3.53%, vs 12.06% for professors).

sentences.en: Professors rated LLMs far higher than their peers (average win rate = 75.33%), with models performing similarly to the best instructor.
sentences.zh: 教授们对法学硕士的评价远高于同行（平均胜率 = 75.33%），模型的表现与最好的导师相似。

sentences.en: LLM responses were also rarely flagged as harmful (3.53%, vs 12.06% for professors).
sentences.zh: LLM 的回答也很少被标记为有害（3.53%，而教授为 12.06%）。

blocks.html_en: This reminds me of the distinction I mentioned in a recent fragment between interactional and contributory expertise.

sentences.en: This reminds me of the distinction I mentioned in a recent fragment between interactional and contributory expertise.
sentences.zh: 这让我想起我在最近的一个片段中提到的互动专业知识和贡献专业知识之间的区别。

blocks.html_en: A few days ago Unmesh Joshi published an article here about his experiences using DSLs to enable more reliable use of LLMs. Responses to this included a pointer to an article by Spender Nelson that related similar impressions.

sentences.en: A few days ago Unmesh Joshi published an article here about his experiences using DSLs to enable more reliable use of LLMs.
sentences.zh: 几天前，Unmesh Joshi 在这里发表了一篇文章，介绍了他使用 DSL 来更可靠地使用 LLM 的经验。

sentences.en: Responses to this included a pointer to an article by Spender Nelson that related similar impressions.
sentences.zh: 对此的回应包括指向 Spender Nelson 的一篇文章，该文章涉及类似的印象。

blocks.html_en:
DSLs like this hit a lot of sweet spots for LLMs. You can make them extremely token efficient, and enforce hard security boundaries. You can translate high-level LLM intent into a ton of deterministic code, ensuring good behavior and guardrails at the (custom) compiler level.

And Large Language Models are very good at learning and working with DSLs. Maybe this shouldn’t come as a surprise; they are language models after all. A small bit of documentation generally is enough to set them off and running, and reasonable error messages let them course-correct even when they go wrong.

sentences.en: DSLs like this hit a lot of sweet spots for LLMs.
sentences.zh: 像这样的 DSL 对法学硕士来说有很多好处。

sentences.en: You can make them extremely token efficient, and enforce hard security boundaries.
sentences.zh: 您可以使它们极其高效，并强制实施严格的安全边界。

sentences.zh: 您可以将高级 LLM 意图转换为大量确定性代码，确保（自定义）编译器级别的良好行为和防护。

sentences.en: And Large Language Models are very good at learning and working with DSLs.
sentences.zh: 大型语言模型非常擅长学习和使用 DSL。

sentences.en: Maybe this shouldn’t come as a surprise; they are language models after all.
sentences.zh: 也许这并不令人意外。毕竟它们是语言模型。

sentences.en: A small bit of documentation generally is enough to set them off and running, and reasonable error messages let them course-correct even when they go wrong.
sentences.zh: 通常，一小部分文档就足以让它们启动并运行，并且合理的错误消息可以让它们即使在出错时也能纠正错误。

blocks.html_en: He describes a couple of examples from their use: a query language for data lakes that takes into account security and authorization issues, and a little expression language to make it easier to create safe SQL where clauses.

sentences.en: He describes a couple of examples from their use: a query language for data lakes that takes into account security and authorization issues, and a little expression language to make it easier to create safe SQL where clauses.
sentences.zh: 他描述了它们的使用示例：一种考虑安全和授权问题的数据湖查询语言，以及一种可以更轻松地创建安全 SQL where 子句的小型表达式语言。

blocks.html_en: One of the biggest barriers to using DSLs, particularly external DSLs, is building a parser and tooling. LLMs make this much easier. That said, my sense is that it’s the semantic model that underpins the DSL is what really matters, and the DSL is one projection of that model. LLMs may help us explore other ways to project that model in interesting ways.

sentences.en: One of the biggest barriers to using DSLs, particularly external DSLs, is building a parser and tooling.
sentences.zh: 使用 DSL（尤其是外部 DSL）的最大障碍之一是构建解析器和工具。

sentences.en: LLMs make this much easier.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士使这变得容易得多。

sentences.en: That said, my sense is that it’s the semantic model that underpins the DSL is what really matters, and the DSL is one projection of that model.
sentences.zh: 也就是说，我的感觉是，真正重要的是支撑 DSL 的语义模型，而 DSL 是该模型的一种投影。

sentences.zh: 法学硕士可以帮助我们探索以有趣的方式投影该模型的其他方法。

blocks.html_en: In recent weeks I’ve been noticing the stench of LLM-speak more and more. It’s not just the common tells, it’s a sense of LLM miasma that pervades the prose. I’ve noticed it’s increasingly eliciting a visceral reaction, after a couple of paragraphs I just want to dismiss the entire article out of hand. For some of these, it was necessary for me to hold my nose and wade through the whole text, but it was with an intellectual nausea which obscured the content, even increasing my desire to indulge in such an awful distraction as checking social media.

sentences.en: In recent weeks I’ve been noticing the stench of LLM-speak more and more.
sentences.zh: 最近几周，我越来越注意到法学硕士的恶臭。

sentences.en: It’s not just the common tells, it’s a sense of LLM miasma that pervades the prose.
sentences.zh: 这不仅仅是常见的说法，而是散文中弥漫着法学硕士的瘴气。

sentences.en: I’ve noticed it’s increasingly eliciting a visceral reaction, after a couple of paragraphs I just want to dismiss the entire article out of hand.
sentences.zh: 我注意到它越来越引起本能的反应，在几段之后我只想立即驳回整篇文章。

sentences.en: For some of these, it was necessary for me to hold my nose and wade through the whole text, but it was with an intellectual nausea which obscured the content, even increasing my desire to indulge in such an awful distraction as checking social media.
sentences.zh: 对于其中一些内容，我有必要捂着鼻子费力地读完整篇文章，但这是一种智力上的恶心，它模糊了内容，甚至增加了我沉迷于查看社交媒体这样可怕的分心的欲望。

blocks.html_en: I wonder - is this just me that’s reacting so negatively to LLM-speak? Or do other people have a reaction that leads them to toss aside any prose that sets off their LLM-alarm?

sentences.en: I wonder - is this just me that’s reacting so negatively to LLM-speak?
sentences.zh: 我想知道——只有我对法学硕士的反应如此消极吗？

sentences.en: Or do other people have a reaction that leads them to toss aside any prose that sets off their LLM-alarm?
sentences.zh: 或者其他人的反应是否会导致他们抛弃任何引起法学硕士警报的散文？

blocks.html_en: One indicator that it’s not just me is this post from Jason Koebler that I highlighted a couple of months ago, where he observed how AI was breaking his brain:

sentences.en: One indicator that it’s not just me is this post from Jason Koebler that I highlighted a couple of months ago, where he observed how AI was breaking his brain:
sentences.zh: 几个月前我强调过 Jason Koebler 的这篇文章，其中一个迹象表明，这不仅仅是我一个人的问题，他在其中观察到人工智能如何打破他的大脑：

blocks.html_en: People think things that are fake are real, things that are real are fake. Much has been written about “AI psychosis,” the nonspecific, nonscientific diagnosis given to people who have lost themselves to AI. Less has been said about the cognitive load of what other people’s AI use is doing to the rest of us, and the insidious nature of having to navigate an internet and a world where lazy AI has infiltrated everything. Our brains are now performing untold numbers of calculations per day: Is this AI? Do I care if it’s AI? Why does this sound or look or read so weird? Does this person just write like this? Is this a person at all?

sentences.en: People think things that are fake are real, things that are real are fake.

blocks.html_en: A while ago, I was thinking that it was reasonable for folks who aren’t as committed to writing as I am to use an AI to help polish their prose. Now I’m turning to encouraging writers to reject it. That pervasive LLM-voice is just so common now, my sense is that it discredits the writing even before the reader has a chance to try to understand what is being said. I don’t think it’s good enough to ask the LLM to write a first draft and then tweak it. I’m not sure writers can edit the LLM-ness out of prose once it’s in there. I even worry about asking an LLM to suggest improvements, I think it’s just too easy to accept an LLM’s suggestions, and in the process trigger your readers’ LLM-antibodies.

blocks.html_en: Of course like most problems, it’s also an opportunity. Those who can get a distinctive human voice will get more visibility and credibility. But the question remains of how we can coach people to let out their true personality into their writing. Academic and corporate writing both tended to stifle engaging prose, LLMs are good amplifiers, and they will amplify this stifling. This is an even greater challenge for those for whom English is their second language (or indeed for many of my colleagues, their third or fourth). It’s too easy for me to neglect to think about a difficulty that I’ve never been able to face.

content_html_zh:
在这篇文章中，我将总结第二届软件开发未来静修会的笔记。 但在此之前，我应该指出，有关静修的完整 Thoughtworks 报告现已发布。 他们有五个主要发现：

代码生成不再是瓶颈，验证才是。 “线束工程”正在成为一门独特的、可拥有的学科。 组织正在面临真正的学徒危机。 高管/工程师的期望差距比任何技术限制都带来更大的风险。 遗留现代化是最清晰、最可靠的近期价值池。

 ❄                ❄

一场会议围绕使用法学硕士的工程师与要求使用法学硕士的最高管理层和董事会之间的观点不匹配而召开。 令人担忧的是，董事会只关注承诺的生产力提升，而对风险，尤其是安全风险，不够关心。

一家公司使用经过机器学习训练的软件来优化现场设备上空气过滤器的更换，这说明了这一点。 他们很高兴看到能够减少更换空气过滤器的频率，从而节省了 5000 万美元。 但问题是机器学习模型是在沙漠中使用的设备上进行训练的，而他们的设备是在北极使用的。 沙漠中的空气过滤器可以处理灰尘，但在北极，要去除的是蚊子。 这里有一个重要的区别，蚊子会腐烂，而足够多的腐烂蚊子会带来严重的火灾风险。 由于不经常更换的空气过滤器周围的死蚊子引发的火灾给该公司造成了 1000 亿美元的损失。

现在，这样的故事可以讲述许多没有人工智能参与的情况。 当在新的环境中应用解决方案时，许多人类情况都会出错（这就是为什么环境是模式编写者的关键词）。 但这个故事确实提醒我们要警惕人工智能的建议，并始终思考如何构建传感器来提供快速反馈。

当公民开发人员开始进行氛围编码时，工程师尤其担心风险。 当然，从很多方面来说，这并不新鲜。 它。 人们经常担心有多少重要的业务决策是基于电子表格的，而这些决策是在几乎没有对数据质量进行控制、测试或评估的情况下构建的。 Vibe 编码加剧了这些担忧，因此公司需要一系列控制措施来防范安全漏洞。 有些人特意在董事会层面提出问题，与董事会成员举行威胁建模会议，向他们介绍风险。 Vibe 编码的应用程序需要放置在单独的基础设施中，该基础设施对数据访问进行确定性控制，以控制致命的三连击。 一家公司鼓励公民开发人员广泛进行振动编码，但对出现的巨大影子 IT 问题感到退缩 - 他们现在正在寻求构建一个平台来帮助控制这项工作，同时又不会扼杀所产生的有用工具。

这里的部分问题可能是法学硕士的简单经验。 许多管理人员发现法学硕士在准备管理报告方面做得很好。 或总结其他法学硕士准备的管理报告。 鉴于此，他们自然认为法学硕士也必须在编程方面做得很好。 我的反管理自我不得不提到凯尔西·海塔尔的观察：

你的工作越不忙，这些人工智能工具的吸引力就越小

一种可能的解决方法是：让法律部门参与其中。 他们看到法学硕士做得很差，并意识到其中涉及的风险。

 ❄                ❄

与我交谈过的大多数人，无论是在静修处还是在外面，都认识到我们正处于某种形式的泡沫之中。 像这样的技术进步几乎总是伴随着经济泡沫，未来我们都会回顾这一点，摇头说我们知道有这么多泡沫。 但是，虽然很容易看出泡沫的存在，但很难看出泡沫会持续多久或泡沫破灭后会出现什么。 毕竟互联网泡沫已被清楚地认识到…… 1995年。 我们可以高兴地指出那些失败的公司（Webvan、pets.com），但需要承认那些幸存下来的公司（亚马逊）。

参加静修会的大多数人年龄都足够大，经历过互联网泡沫和崩溃，但其中一位白发人士指出了一个有趣的差异。 当时我们对未来会发生什么感到兴奋，我们看到了许多新事物正在建造。 这次，这样的事情要少得多。 大多数人对人工智能泡沫所造成的后果持谨慎态度。 这在一定程度上可能源于互联网公司希望所带来的现实。 社交媒体可能无处不在，但我们认为它实际上改善了我们的生活吗，即使（特别是如果？） 我们用了这么多吗？

我们听说过很多关于使用代理编程可以实现的令人难以置信的高效事情，但是有人注意到用它构建了大量精彩的应用程序吗？ 或者我们是否注意到谷歌或微软等大型人工智能助推器对常见应用程序的显着改进？

这可能是董事会与工程师分歧的另一个因素。 目前推动人工智能采用的大部分因素是成本削减，而董事会对成本削减感到兴奋。 也许对代币成本日益增加的担忧会缓和这种渴望。

 ❄                ❄

人们发现法学硕士对运营很有帮助：通过可观察性工具提供的良好事件流，代理可以更快地发现异常情况。 公民开发者应用程序的问题之一是它们通常不能提供良好的可观察性，因为公民开发者不认为需要它。 代理查看事件流的能力确实会带来治理问题，因为此类事件流通常包含大量敏感信息。

更多的人同意法学硕士对于运营人员来说很有价值，可以帮助他们理解代码的作用，这证实了我在犹他州听到的说法。 交叉匹配代码和事件跟踪可以帮助人们在出现问题时找到发生的情况。 特工在处理重复事件时特别得心应手，因为他们可以整理来自不同案例的大量信息并将其呈现给人类团队。

让代理自动修复将我们的能力和关注点提升到一个新的水平。 代理商在进行修复时仔细记录他们的所有操作，这一点至关重要。 我们还需要确保向开发团队提供反馈，以便他们可以学习。 代理不会学习，他们能做的最好的事情就是更新上下文。

有一种感觉，许多人高估了特工处理事件的能力。 这些人认为事件解决是一个简单的线性过程。 但这种情况很少发生，相反，需要很多惊喜和适应。 人类很擅长这一点，但法学硕士却不然。

代理开发代码的危险之一是他们习惯于插入从未被要求的功能。 一个团队花了三天时间试图找出这样一个未被请求的功能，试图找出谁请求了它以及是否有人想保留它。

 ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄

一群法学教授进行了一项有趣的实验，以判断法学硕士能够在多大程度上为学生的问题提供简短的答案。 他们创建了一批四十个合同法问题，并要求教授和几位法学硕士提供答案。 为了评估法学硕士的答案，他们向教授展示了一对答案——一个人，一个法学硕士——并询问他们更愿意向学生提供哪一个答案。

教授们对法学硕士的评价远高于同行（平均胜率 = 75.33%），模型的表现与最好的导师相似。 LLM 的回答也很少被标记为有害（3.53%，而教授为 12.06%）。

这让我想起我在最近的一个片段中提到的互动专业知识和贡献专业知识之间的区别。

 ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄

几天前，Unmesh Joshi 在这里发表了一篇文章，介绍了他使用 DSL 来更可靠地使用 LLM 的经验。 对此的回应包括指向 Spender Nelson 的一篇文章，该文章涉及类似的印象。

像这样的 DSL 对法学硕士来说有很多好处。 您可以使它们极其高效，并强制实施严格的安全边界。 您可以将高级 LLM 意图转换为大量确定性代码，确保（自定义）编译器级别的良好行为和防护。 大型语言模型非常擅长学习和使用 DSL。 也许这并不令人意外。毕竟它们是语言模型。 通常，一小部分文档就足以让它们启动并运行，并且合理的错误消息可以让它们即使在出错时也能纠正错误。

他描述了它们的使用示例：一种考虑安全和授权问题的数据湖查询语言，以及一种可以更轻松地创建安全 SQL where 子句的小型表达式语言。

使用 DSL（尤其是外部 DSL）的最大障碍之一是构建解析器和工具。 法学硕士使这变得容易得多。 也就是说，我的感觉是，真正重要的是支撑 DSL 的语义模型，而 DSL 是该模型的一种投影。 法学硕士可以帮助我们探索以有趣的方式投影该模型的其他方法。

 ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄                ❄

最近几周，我越来越注意到法学硕士的恶臭。 这不仅仅是常见的说法，而是散文中弥漫着法学硕士的瘴气。 我注意到它越来越引起本能的反应，在几段之后我只想立即驳回整篇文章。 对于其中一些内容，我有必要捂着鼻子费力地读完整篇文章，但这是一种智力上的恶心，它模糊了内容，甚至增加了我沉迷于查看社交媒体这样可怕的分心的欲望。

我想知道——只有我对法学硕士的反应如此消极吗？ 或者其他人的反应是否会导致他们抛弃任何引起法学硕士警报的散文？

几个月前我强调过 Jason Koebler 的这篇文章，其中一个迹象表明，这不仅仅是我一个人的问题，他在其中观察到人工智能如何打破他的大脑：

人们认为假的东西是真的，真实的东西是假的。 关于“人工智能精神病”的文章已经有很多，这是一种针对那些因人工智能而迷失自我的人的非特异性、非科学的诊断。 关于其他人的人工智能使用对我们其他人造成的认知负担，以及必须在互联网和懒惰的人工智能已经渗透到一切的世界中导航的阴险本质，人们很少说。 我们的大脑现在每天执行无数的计算：这是人工智能吗？ 我关心它是否是人工智能吗？ 为什么这听起来、看起来或读起来如此奇怪？ 这个人就这样写吗？ 这到底是一个人吗？

不久前，我在想，对于那些不像我一样致力于写作的人来说，使用人工智能来帮助润色他们的散文是合理的。 现在我转而鼓励作家拒绝它。 这种普遍的法学硕士声音现在非常普遍，我的感觉是，甚至在读者有机会尝试理解所讲内容之前，它就已经使写作失去了可信度。 我认为要求法学硕士写出初稿然后进行调整还不够好。 我不确定作家们是否可以在散文中编辑出法学硕士的内容。 我什至担心要求法学硕士提出改进建议，我认为接受法学硕士的建议太容易了，并在这个过程中触发了读者的法学硕士抗体。

当然，像大多数问题一样，这也是一个机会。 那些能够获得独特的人声的人将获得更多的知名度和可信度。 但问题仍然是我们如何指导人们在写作中展现他们的真实个性。 学术和企业写作都倾向于抑制引人入胜的散文，法学硕士是很好的放大器，他们会放大这种抑制。 对于那些以英语为第二语言（或者对于我的许多同事来说，第三或第四语言）的人来说，这是一个更大的挑战。 对我来说，我很容易忽视去思考我从未面对过的困难。

我能给出的最直接的建议是我多年前学到并去年分享的东西——说出你的写作。

一旦你有了合理的草稿，就大声朗读。 通过这样做，你会发现听起来不正确的地方，需要修复。

我总是建议这样做，以帮助人们摆脱缓慢的散文，特别是如果他们花了太多时间在学术或企业写作上。 但现在我认为为了对抗人工智能的潜在影响，说出你的写作的必要性更加重要。 对于大多数人来说，他们的言语模式更接近真实的自我，因此语言化写作是对抗那些试图消除作家个性的力量的方法。
